1: Welcome. This is a calm walkthrough ahead of your birth, meant to make what lies ahead less unknown and more reassuring. Take it at your own pace.
2: We start with how you notice it is time, go through the stages of labour, the room, and who is there. Then pain relief, breathing, affirmations, and Jocke's role. Stop and ask whenever you like.
3: There are a few signs that it is getting close: contractions that come closer together, longer, and stronger, your waters breaking, or the mucus plug coming away. Pre-labour contractions are irregular and of varying length, and can settle with rest. Active contractions carry on no matter what you do. A rule of thumb is to go in when the contractions come every four minutes, last about a minute, and have done so for an hour. Or straight away if your waters break or you feel worried.
4: Labour is usually divided into three stages. First the dilation stage, when the cervix softens and opens all the way to ten centimetres. Then the pushing stage, when you bear down and the baby is born. And finally the placental stage, when the placenta comes, shortly after the baby.
5: The birth room is usually calmer than people think. The bed can be set in different positions, a monitor listens to the baby's heartbeat now and then, and many rooms have a ball, a shower, or a bath. The lighting can be dimmed, and there is room for Jocke. You get to influence the light, the music, and who is in the room, so say what you want.
6: In the room there is always a midwife, who leads the birth and receives the baby, and an assistant nurse who assists and is there for you. If needed, a doctor comes in, a paediatrician for the baby or an anaesthetist for an epidural, for example. The midwife comes and goes. You are never alone with the responsibility, but neither are you watched every second.
7: The latent phase is the warm-up. The cervix softens and opens to around three or four centimetres, and the contractions are still irregular. This phase can go on for hours, sometimes up to a whole day the first time. Stay at home as long as it feels safe, eat lightly, drink, and rest if you can. Jocke creates calm, keeps track of the timings, and makes sure Felicia gets enough to drink.
8: In the active phase the contractions become regular, strong, and closer together, and the dilation goes from about four to ten centimetres. Now you are usually with the midwife. Felicia, find your rhythm with breathing and movement, one contraction at a time. Jocke, stay close: breathe with her, give water between contractions, massage her lower back, and be her voice with the staff.
9: The transition phase is the short, tough bit just before the pushing. This is often when the doubt comes, the feeling of not being able to go on. It is actually a sign that it is about to turn. Jocke, don't back off now: calm voice, eye contact, and remind her that she is already doing this.
10: Then comes a strong, irresistible urge to push. You follow your body and the midwife's guidance, pushing during the contraction and relaxing in between. It can take from a few minutes to a good hour. Jocke supports her upper body, holds her hand, and breathes along. Soon you meet your baby.
11: Right after the birth the baby is placed skin to skin on Felicia's chest, for warmth, security, and often the first breastfeed. The placenta is delivered with a mild contraction shortly after, usually barely noticeable. Jocke is welcome to cut the umbilical cord and take skin to skin too. The first minutes are just the three of you.
12: Prophylaxis is simple tools you practise in advance, so they are there automatically when it counts. Breathe in calmly through the nose and out longer through the mouth. A contraction is just a few deep breaths. Let go of the tension in your shoulders, jaw, and hands, and rest your gaze on a point or on Jocke's voice. Practise together a few times beforehand, so the calm settles in your body.
13: There is plenty of pain relief without medication: moving and changing position, warmth from a shower, a wheat bag, or a bath, massage and pressure on the lower back, the breathing you have practised, TENS, and sterile water injections for back pain. A calm environment with dimmed light and a sense of safety actually lowers the experience of pain itself.
14: And then there is medication. Laughing gas, which you breathe during the contraction to take the edge off. The epidural, EDA, which is the most powerful and is given by an anaesthetist. A pudendal block late in the process. And morphine-related drugs that can be given early for rest. None of it is cheating. The right pain relief is the one that helps you, and you are allowed to change your mind along the way.
15: Affirmations are words that carry you. A few to say to yourself, and a few that Jocke can say to you. Press the play buttons and you will hear how they can sound.
16: Jocke's most important place is by her face, at eye level. That is where you are most useful, for the breathing, the eye contact, and a calm voice. Stand beside or behind her in standing positions and press on her lower back, and be by her upper body during the pushing. Keep out of the midwife's way, and you don't have to look downwards if you don't want to. Eat, drink, and sit down when you need to, you help no one by fainting.
17: Whatever the phase: be present and put the phone down. Breathe with her, one contraction at a time. Have water, snacks, and a cool towel ready. Use a calm voice, pass on her wishes to the staff, and keep your own calm by eating, drinking, and breathing yourself too.
18: Sometimes a little help along the way is needed, and that is common and safe. It may be a drip to strengthen the contractions if they ease off, a ventouse for the last bit, or a caesarean, planned or emergency. The staff explain what and why, and you get to be part of it. A different way in does not mean a worse birth.
19: Pack the bag in good time. For Felicia: comfortable clothes, a nursing top, slippers, toiletries, and a charger with a long cord. For the baby: bodysuits, sleepsuits, a hat, and a blanket, and the car seat ready in the car. For Jocke: a change of clothes, food and drink, a card, and patience.
20: The first hours afterwards are skin to skin and the first breastfeed. Felicia rests, eats, and passes urine, which is important. The baby is examined, and you get time together before you move to the postnatal ward or go home. It feels unreal, emotional, and exhausting all at once. Completely normal.
21: You can do this. Trust your body, each other, and the staff around you. If you have questions, just ask them.
